food_subgroup: Pan-fried rice flour cake with condiment sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for chai tow kway or radish cake. FOODON_03302051 (fried food product) used as closest parent. Mala chilli dipping sauce component has no separate FoodOn entry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW (Grain-based mixed dishes) is the closest defensible parent for this rice-flour-based pan-fried cake. No specific code for chai tow kway exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chai tow kway or radish cake with mala sauce.

main_ingredients: rice flour radish cake (chai tow kway)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker or restaurant serving of carrot cake with mala chilli dipping sauce is approximately 300 g, comprising a plate of pan-fried radish cake cubes with egg and a side of mala chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chai Tow Kway (Fried Carrot Cake) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Chai Tow Kway (fried carrot cake, white variety) per 100 g. The mala chilli dipping sauce component contributes additional sodium and fat; an upward adjustment of approximately 10–15% to sodium and fat values has been applied to reflect the mala sauce component, consistent with HPB condiment data for chilli-based sauces. Energy and carbohydrate values are anchored to the HPB Chai Tow Kway entry. This is an adapted estimate; exact nutrient values for this specific restaurant preparation are not independently verified.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice flour-based steamed and fried cake analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chai tow kway with mala sauce. GI of 52 is estimated by analogy to rice flour-based fried cakes and steamed rice products (GI range 48–56) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Pan-frying and egg coating lower the effective GI relative to plain steamed rice. Mala sauce contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter GI. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 58.5) / 100 ≈ 30.4 for a 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from preserved radish, soy sauce, and mala chilli sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 1,560 mg sodium, representing over 67% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced sauce or omit the mala dipping sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) and significant carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 30 per standard serving, this dish can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (150–200 g), pair with non-starchy vegetables, and avoid the mala sauce if it contains added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with pan-frying oil, egg cholesterol, and high sodium from mala sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of pan-frying fat (approximately 8.5 g fat per 100 g), dietary cholesterol from egg (approximately 82 mg per 100 g), and high sodium makes this dish a moderate cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose smaller portions without the mala dipping sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carrot Cake with Mala Chilli Dipping Sauce is a Singaporean hawker dish based on chai tow kway — a savoury pan-fried cake made from steamed rice flour and white radish (daikon), cut into cubes and stir-fried with egg, preserved radish (chai poh), spring onion, and soy sauce. Despite its English name, this dish contains no carrot; the name derives from the Hokkien term for white radish (chai tow). The mala chilli dipping sauce is a Sichuan-influenced condiment featuring dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, and oil, delivering a numbing (ma) and spicy (la) flavour profile. The dish has a crispy exterior with a soft, chewy interior, and the mala sauce adds intense heat and tingling spice. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal per 100 g) with a significant sodium load (~520 mg per 100 g) primarily from preserved radish, soy sauce, and the mala sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), though the glycemic load per standard serving is substantial (~30). It is a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to carbohydrate load, and a high concern for hypertension due to sodium. The dish is not suitable for halal diets without verified halal-certified ingredients. It is a popular zi char and hawker centre item in Singapore, also served in upscale casual dining settings such as Flavours by Saute.

confidence_reason: Base nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for chai tow kway (fried carrot cake). The mala chilli dipping sauce component required an upward sodium and fat adjustment using HPB condiment reference data. No exact HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific combined dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice flour-based fried cakes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for chai tow kway. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Grains and staples', as chai tow kway is a rice-flour-based staple dish, not a condiment. The mala sauce is a condiment component but does not define the food group of the composite dish. 5. The cuisine has been corrected from 'Global' to 'Singaporean' based on the dish's established origin as a Singaporean hawker food. 6. The input cuisine_tags (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) are irrelevant to this dish and have been discarded. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chai tow kway with mala sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain non-halal soy sauce or lard in some preparations; the specific restaurant (Flavours by Saute - Funan) has not been verified as halal-certified.
